preFix.title: Adjusting pig feed from weaner to finisher
preFix.subtitle: Matching the ration to the pig's stage of growth from 7 kg to slaughter weight.
preFix.excerpt: Pigs kept on the same ration from weaning to slaughter put on more fat than meat in the final weeks. Understanding the three-stage feed progression, weaner to grower to finisher, keeps the carcass lean and the feed cost down. The shift in ration is simple; the timing is based on liveweight, not age.
preFix.sourceNotes:
AHDB Pork, Pig Feed Management Guide (2022). https://ahdb.org.uk
DEFRA Code of Recommendations for the Welfare of Pigs (2003, Crown copyright). https://www.gov.uk/government/collections/animal-welfare-codes-of-practice
Carla Emery, Encyclopedia of Country Living (Sasquatch Books, 12th ed. 2012), pig chapter on growth and feeding.

content.text: A
weaner

arriving on the holding is building frame and muscle. A pig at 90 kg is starting to lay fat. The feed that suits one stage does not suit the other, and the difference in ration is one of the most straightforward practical changes a smallholder can make to improve the final carcass.
The three stages
Pig feed is broadly divided into three growth stages. The boundaries are liveweight, not age, because pigs grow at different rates depending on breed, sex, and feed quality.
Weaner feed: from arrival at 7 to 10 kg through to 30 kg. High protein (around 20 percent crude protein), small pellet size,
ad-lib feeding
. Focus is on gut development and frame.
Grower feed: from 30 kg to 60 kg. Around 18 percent crude protein, larger pellet, ad-lib or controlled feeding. Focus is on lean muscle development.
Finisher feed: from 60 kg to slaughter weight, typically 90 to 110 kg. Around 15 to 16 percent crude protein, controlled feeding twice daily. Focus is on carcass quality and lean-to-fat ratio.
Weaner stage

Newly arrived weaners are sometimes reluctant to eat in the first 48 hours. This is normal. Do not switch feed immediately if they arrived on a different product; keep the same feed for the first two weeks and transition to your preferred weaner ration over a further two weeks by mixing old and new feed in increasing proportions. A sudden feed change in a weaner causes digestive upset and scours.
Weaner pellets are small and dust-free. Keep the feed station covered so the feed does not get wet; wet feed ferments and the pigs go off it. The
grower ration
transition begins around 30 kg, which for most commercial hybrid breeds is at about 10 to 12 weeks old.
Grower stage

The grower stage is the most straightforward. The pigs are robust, weather-tolerant, and taking on weight quickly. Ad-lib feeding through a covered hopper is the standard approach. Check and refill the hopper daily. A pig that runs out of feed for more than a few hours compensates by overeating when feed returns, which causes digestive upsets.

Watch for bullying at the feeder. In a two-pig pair, one animal often gets less feed than the other. Two separate feed stations prevents this. In a group of four, three feed stations are a practical minimum.
Finisher stage
Introduce the
finisher ration
gradually when the pigs reach approximately 60 kg. Switch over seven to ten days by mixing increasing proportions of finisher with grower pellets.

At the finisher stage, many smallholders move from ad-lib to twice-daily controlled feeding. The reason is fat deposition: a pig with unrestricted access to high-energy feed in the final weeks lays down more backfat than most home butchers want. Feed twice a day at a fixed rate, typically 2 to 2.5 kg per pig per day for a standard hybrid at this stage. The pigs will be hungry; this is by design.

attrs.body: Supplementing the finisher ration with kitchen scraps is legal in the UK only if the scraps are vegetable-based and do not contain meat, fish, or dairy. Any meat or animal-product scraps fed to pigs (including cooking water that has touched meat) are classified as catering waste and are illegal as pig feed. This applies even on a single-pig smallholding. Forage from the paddock, rooting in a grass run, is unrestricted.
attrs.title: Kitchen scraps and foraging at the finisher stage

items.fix: Slow the transition. Mix old and new feed 50:50 for a week, then 25:75 before moving to full new feed. Scours in weaner pigs that persist past 48 hours after a feed change need attention.
items.cause: Feed transition too fast or new feed contains a higher fibre content than the pigs are used to
items.symptom: Pigs scouring (loose, watery droppings) after a feed change

items.fix: Separate the smaller pig and feed it independently for the remainder of the finisher stage. The larger animal will reach slaughter weight first; the smaller one will follow two to three weeks later.
items.cause: Unequal access to feed through the grower stage
items.symptom: One pig significantly smaller than the other at the finisher stage

items.fix: At the finisher stage with controlled feeding, some restlessness before feeds is normal. A fixed galvanised steel trough bolted to the fence post eliminates tipping. Ensure feed times are consistent; pigs learn the schedule and settle.
items.cause: Bored, hungry, or the feeder is poorly secured
items.symptom: Pigs eating the feeder, rooting it out, and tipping it over
